news_summary: Scientists have achieved major anti-aging breakthroughs by rejuvenating old blood stem cells, reversing biological age through diet, decoding a rare cancer-fighting plant compound, and discovering a fat-burning switch that may also strengthen bones.
news_summary_fr: Les scientifiques ont réalisé des avancées majeures dans la lutte contre le vieillissement en rajeunissant des cellules souches sanguines âgées, en inversant l'âge biologique grâce à l'alimentation, en décryptant un composé végétal rare aux propriétés anticancéreuses et en découvrant un mécanisme de combustion des graisses qui pourrait également renforcer les os.
news_summary_es: Los científicos han logrado importantes avances en la lucha contra el envejecimiento al rejuvenecer células madre sanguíneas envejecidas, revertir la edad biológica mediante la dieta, descifrar un compuesto vegetal poco común con propiedades anticancerígenas y descubrir un mecanismo que quema grasa y que también podría fortalecer los huesos.

articles.title: Scientists make old blood stem cells young again in major anti-aging breakthrough
articles.summary: As blood stem cells age, their lysosomes become overactive and damaged, triggering inflammation and weakening the body’s ability to regenerate healthy blood and immune cells. By calming this cellular “overdrive,” researchers restored the stem cells’ youthful function, dramatically boosting their ability to regenerate and produce balanced blood cells.
articles.content:
Scientists at the Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai have successfully reversed aging in blood-forming stem cells in mice by repairing defects in structures known as lysosomes. The findings, published in Cell Stem Cell, point to lysosomal dysfunction and overactivity as major causes of stem cell aging and show that restoring proper lysosomal activity can rejuvenate old stem cells and improve their ability to regenerate blood and immune cells.

Lysosomes function as the cell's internal recycling centers. They break down proteins, nucleic acids, carbohydrates, and lipids, helping cells dispose of waste and reuse materials for essential biological processes. They also store nutrients that can be released when needed. Because of these roles, lysosomes are critical for maintaining cellular metabolism, including both catabolism (breaking down complex molecules to simple ones) and anabolism (building complex molecules from simpler ones).

The research team focused on hematopoietic stem cells (HSCs), which are rare, long-lasting stem cells found in the bone marrow that generate all blood and immune cells in the body. The study was led by Saghi Ghaffari, MD, PhD, Professor of Cell, Developmental, and Regenerative Biology at the Icahn School of Medicine and a member of the Black Family Stem Cell Institute.

As people grow older, these stem cells gradually lose their ability to repair and replenish the blood system. This decline weakens immune defenses and contributes to the increased vulnerability to infections seen in older adults. Aging HSCs are also linked to clonal hematopoiesis, an asymptomatic condition considered a premalignant state that raises the risk of blood cancers and inflammatory diseases. The condition becomes much more common with age.

According to the American Cancer Society, age and smoking are the two strongest risk factors associated with the five-year risk of developing cancer. Data from the National Cancer Institute's Surveillance, Epidemiology, and End Results report show that the median age at cancer diagnosis is 67.

Restoring Old Stem Cells to a Youthful State

The researchers discovered that lysosomes in aged HSCs become excessively acidic, damaged, depleted, and abnormally active. These changes disrupt both the metabolic balance and epigenetic stability of the stem cells.

Using single-cell transcriptomics and functional testing, the team found that blocking this excessive lysosomal activity with a vacuolar ATPase inhibitor restored lysosomal health and improved the function of aging blood stem cells.

After treatment, the old stem cells began behaving more like young, healthy cells again. They regained the ability to regenerate effectively, produce balanced blood and immune cells, and generate additional healthy stem cells. The treated cells also showed improved metabolism and mitochondrial performance, healthier epigenetic patterns, reduced inflammation, and fewer harmful inflammatory signals that can damage tissues throughout the body.

"Our findings reveal that aging in blood stem cells is not an irreversible fate. Old blood stem cells have the capacity to revert to a youthful state; they can bounce back," said Dr. Ghaffari. "By slowing down the lysosomes and reducing their acidity, stem cells became healthier and could make new balanced blood cells and new stem cells much more effectively. By targeting lysosomal hyperactivity, we were able to reset aged stem cells to a younger, healthier state, improving their ability to regenerate blood and immune cells."

Major Increase in Blood-Forming Capacity

The researchers also tested an ex vivo treatment approach (when cells are removed from the body, modified in a laboratory, and returned to the body). Treating old stem cells with the lysosomal inhibitor increased their blood-forming ability in living animals by more than eightfold, highlighting the powerful regenerative effects of correcting lysosomal dysfunction.

The improvement also reduced damaging inflammatory and interferon-related pathways. According to the researchers, this occurred because healthier lysosomes improved the processing of mitochondrial DNA and lowered activation of the cGAS-STING immune signaling pathway, which appears to play a major role in stem cell inflammation and aging.

Potential for Anti-Aging and Blood Disorder Therapies

The findings could open the door to new treatments aimed at preventing or reversing age-related blood disorders. They may also improve stem cell transplantation outcomes in older patients and enhance conditioning methods used in gene therapy.

"Lysosomal dysfunction emerges as a central driver of stem cell aging," added Dr. Ghaffari. "Targeting this pathway may one day help maintain healthy blood and immune systems in the elderly, improve their stem cells for transplantation, and reduce the risk of age-associated blood disorders and perhaps have an effect on overall aging."

The team is now investigating whether lysosomal dysfunction in aging stem cells contributes to the development of leukemic stem cells, potentially connecting normal stem cell aging with cancer formation.

The research involved collaboration with Mickaël Ménager, PhD, and colleagues at the Imagine Institute and INSERM UMR 1163 at Université de Paris Cité in Paris. Funding was provided by the National Institutes of Health, New York State Stem Cell Science, INSERM, and the Agence Nationale de la Recherche.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in reversing aging in blood-forming stem cells, which has broad implications for improving immune function, reducing age-related blood disorders, and potentially lowering cancer risks. The research is detailed, focused on a single topic, and highlights a tangible advancement with potential large-scale health benefits for the aging population.
articles.personality_title: Scientists reverse aging in blood stem cells to boost regeneration
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Blood-forming stem cells, called hematopoietic stem cells (HSCs), help create all the blood and immune cells in our bodies. As we age, these stem cells lose their ability to regenerate, making it harder for older people to fight infections and increasing risks for blood diseases and cancer.

**What happened** – Researchers at the Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai found that the lysosomes inside aged blood stem cells become damaged and overactive. Lysosomes are parts of cells that recycle waste and manage nutrients. By using a special treatment to calm down these lysosomes, scientists were able to make old stem cells behave like young ones again. This treatment improved the stem cells’ ability to produce healthy blood and immune cells in mice.

**Impact** – This discovery shows that aging in blood stem cells can be reversed, not just slowed down. By fixing lysosome problems, the stem cells worked better, reduced harmful inflammation, and increased their capacity to regenerate blood cells by more than eight times. This could lead to better treatments for older people to keep their immune systems strong and lower risks of blood cancers and other age-related diseases.

**What's next step** – Scientists plan to study if fixing lysosome problems can also stop the formation of cancerous stem cells. They want to explore new therapies that may improve stem cell transplants and gene treatments, especially for older patients.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Repairing lysosomes in aging blood stem cells restores their youthful ability to regenerate healthy blood and immune cells, opening new paths for treating age-related diseases.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques rajeunissent les cellules souches sanguines pour améliorer la régénération
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les cellules souches hématopoïétiques (CSH) produisent toutes les cellules sanguines et immunitaires. En vieillissant, ces cellules perdent leur capacité à se régénérer, ce qui affaiblit le système immunitaire des personnes âgées et augmente les risques de maladies sanguines et de cancers.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’école de médecine Icahn à Mount Sinai ont découvert que les lysosomes des cellules souches sanguines âgées deviennent endommagés et trop actifs. Les lysosomes sont des parties des cellules qui recyclent les déchets et gèrent les nutriments. En calmant ces lysosomes avec un traitement spécial, ils ont réussi à faire revenir ces vieilles cellules à un état plus jeune, améliorant leur capacité à produire des cellules sanguines et immunitaires saines chez la souris.

**Impact** – Cette découverte montre que le vieillissement des cellules souches sanguines peut être inversé. En réparant les lysosomes, les cellules ont mieux fonctionné, réduit l’inflammation nuisible, et augmenté par plus de huit fois leur capacité à régénérer du sang. Cela pourrait permettre de mieux traiter les personnes âgées pour renforcer leur système immunitaire et diminuer les risques de cancers et autres maladies liées à l’âge.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont étudier si la réparation des lysosomes peut aussi empêcher la formation de cellules souches cancéreuses. Ils souhaitent développer de nouvelles thérapies pour améliorer les greffes de cellules souches et les traitements géniques, surtout chez les patients âgés.

**Une phrase clé** – Réparer les lysosomes des cellules souches sanguines âgées restaure leur capacité à régénérer du sang et des cellules immunitaires saines, ouvrant la voie à de nouveaux traitements des maladies liées à l’âge.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos rejuvenecen células madre sanguíneas para mejorar la regeneración
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las células madre hematopoyéticas (HSC) producen todas las células sanguíneas e inmunitarias. Al envejecer, estas células pierden su capacidad de regenerarse, lo que debilita el sistema inmunológico y aumenta el riesgo de enfermedades sanguíneas y cáncer en personas mayores.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Escuela de Medicina Icahn en Mount Sinai descubrieron que los lisosomas en las células madre sanguíneas envejecidas se dañan y se vuelven demasiado activos. Los lisosomas son partes de la célula que reciclan desechos y manejan nutrientes. Al calmar estos lisosomas con un tratamiento especial, lograron que las células viejas funcionaran como jóvenes, mejorando su capacidad para producir células sanguíneas e inmunitarias saludables en ratones.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo demuestra que el envejecimiento de las células madre sanguíneas puede revertirse. Al corregir los problemas de los lisosomas, las células mejoraron su función, redujeron la inflamación dañina y aumentaron su capacidad regenerativa más de ocho veces. Esto podría ayudar a crear mejores tratamientos para mantener fuerte el sistema inmunológico en personas mayores y reducir riesgos de cáncer y otras enfermedades relacionadas con la edad.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos planean investigar si reparar los lisosomas también puede evitar que se formen células madre cancerosas. Quieren desarrollar nuevas terapias para mejorar los trasplantes de células madre y tratamientos genéticos, especialmente en pacientes mayores.

**Una frase clave** – Reparar los lisosomas en células madre sanguíneas envejecidas restaura su capacidad juvenil para regenerar células sanguíneas e inmunitarias saludables, abriendo nuevas vías para tratar enfermedades relacionadas con la edad.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a vibrant, glowing network of blood-forming stem cells inside a softly illuminated bone marrow cavity, with symbolic lysosomes depicted as gentle, balanced recycling centers emitting a calming light that restores and rejuvenates the surrounding cells, all rendered with natural, muted colors and an abstract, cellular landscape style.

articles.title: A rare cancer-fighting plant compound has been decoded
articles.summary: Scientists at UBC Okanagan have uncovered how plants produce mitraphylline, a rare natural compound with promising anti cancer potential. The team identified two enzymes that work together to build the molecule’s unusual twisted structure, solving a mystery that had puzzled researchers for years. Because mitraphylline appears only in tiny amounts in tropical plants like kratom and cat’s claw, the discovery could make it far easier to produce sustainably in the future.
articles.content:
Researchers at UBC Okanagan have uncovered the process plants use to create mitraphylline, a rare natural compound that has attracted attention for its possible cancer fighting properties.

Mitraphylline belongs to a unique class of plant chemicals known as spirooxindole alkaloids. These molecules are recognized for their unusual twisted ring structures and their powerful biological effects, including anti inflammatory and anti tumor activity.

Even though scientists have studied these compounds for years, the exact molecular steps plants use to produce them had remained unknown.

Breakthrough Discovery in Plant Chemistry

That mystery began to unravel in 2023 when Dr. Thu-Thuy Dang's team in UBC Okanagan's Irving K. Barber Faculty of Science identified the first known plant enzyme capable of twisting a molecule into the distinctive spiro shape.

Building on that earlier finding, doctoral student Tuan-Anh Nguyen led new research that uncovered two critical enzymes involved in the production of mitraphylline. One enzyme organizes the molecule into the correct three dimensional structure, while the second transforms it into mitraphylline itself.

"This is similar to finding the missing links in an assembly line," says Dr. Dang, UBC Okanagan Principal's Research Chair in Natural Products Biotechnology. "It answers a long-standing question about how nature builds these complex molecules and gives us a new way to replicate that process."

Why Mitraphylline Is So Valuable

Many promising natural compounds are found only in tiny amounts inside plants, making them difficult and expensive to recreate in laboratories. Mitraphylline is one of those rare substances. It exists only in trace quantities in tropical trees such as Mitragyna (kratom) and Uncaria (cat's claw), both members of the coffee family.

Now that researchers have identified the enzymes responsible for shaping and assembling mitraphylline, they have a clearer path toward producing the compound and related molecules in more sustainable ways.

"With this discovery, we have a green chemistry approach to accessing compounds with enormous pharmaceutical value," says Nguyen. "This is a result of UBC Okanagan's research environment, where students and faculty work closely to solve problems with global reach."

Nguyen also reflected on the experience of contributing to the breakthrough.

"Being part of the team that uncovered the enzymes behind spirooxindole compounds has been amazing," Nguyen adds. "UBC Okanagan's mentorship and support made this possible, and I'm excited to keep growing as a researcher here in Canada."

International Collaboration Fuels the Research

The project brought together Dr. Dang's laboratory at UBC Okanagan and Dr. Satya Nadakuduti's research group at the University of Florida.

Funding for the work came from Canada's Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council's Alliance International Collaboration program, the Canada Foundation for Innovation, and the Michael Smith Health Research BC Scholar Program. Additional support was provided by the United States Department of Agriculture's National Institute of Food and Agriculture.

"We are proud of this discovery coming from UBC Okanagan. Plants are fantastic natural chemists," Dr. Dang says. "Our next steps will focus on adapting their molecular tools to create a wider range of therapeutic compounds."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in understanding how plants produce mitraphylline, a rare compound with promising anti-cancer properties. This discovery enables sustainable production methods, potentially benefiting pharmaceutical development and cancer treatment on a broad scale. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights meaningful positive impact for global health.
articles.personality_title: Scientists uncover how plants make rare cancer-fighting compound
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long been interested in mitraphylline, a rare natural chemical found in tropical plants like kratom and cat’s claw. This compound is known for its unusual twisted shape and potential to fight cancer, but how plants produce it was a mystery.

**What happened** – Researchers at UBC Okanagan discovered two special enzymes that work together to build mitraphylline’s unique structure. Led by doctoral student Tuan-Anh Nguyen and Dr. Thu-Thuy Dang, the team identified how one enzyme shapes the molecule and the other completes its formation. This breakthrough solves a puzzle that had challenged scientists for years.

**Impact** – Mitraphylline is found only in very small amounts in certain plants, making it hard and costly to obtain. Understanding the enzymes involved means scientists can now explore ways to produce mitraphylline more sustainably, without relying on rare plants. This could help develop new medicines using this promising compound.

**What's next step** – The research team plans to use these enzymes to create other useful medicinal compounds. They will work on adapting the natural process to make a wider range of drugs, aiming to improve how these valuable molecules are made in the future.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have decoded how plants make mitraphylline, opening the door to sustainable production of this rare cancer-fighting compound.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques découvrent comment les plantes fabriquent un composé rare contre le cancer
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques s'intéressent depuis longtemps à la mitraphylline, un composé naturel rare présent dans des plantes tropicales comme le kratom et l'ongle du chat. Ce composé a une forme torsadée unique et pourrait combattre le cancer, mais la façon dont les plantes le produisent restait inconnue.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs de l'UBC Okanagan ont découvert deux enzymes spéciales qui travaillent ensemble pour construire la structure unique de la mitraphylline. Dirigée par l'étudiant en doctorat Tuan-Anh Nguyen et la Dre Thu-Thuy Dang, l'équipe a identifié comment une enzyme façonne la molécule et l'autre complète sa formation. Cette avancée résout une énigme qui défiait les scientifiques depuis des années.

**Impact** – La mitraphylline se trouve en très petites quantités dans certaines plantes, ce qui la rend difficile et coûteuse à obtenir. Comprendre les enzymes impliquées permet maintenant d'explorer des méthodes de production plus durables, sans dépendre des plantes rares. Cela pourrait aider à développer de nouveaux médicaments utilisant ce composé prometteur.

**Prochaine étape** – L'équipe de recherche prévoit d'utiliser ces enzymes pour créer d'autres composés médicinaux utiles. Ils travailleront à adapter ce processus naturel pour fabriquer une plus grande variété de médicaments, dans le but d'améliorer la production de ces molécules précieuses.

**Phrase clé** – Les scientifiques ont déchiffré comment les plantes fabriquent la mitraphylline, ouvrant la voie à une production durable de ce composé rare contre le cancer.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren cómo las plantas producen un compuesto raro contra el cáncer
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han estado interesados durante mucho tiempo en la mitrafílina, un compuesto natural raro que se encuentra en plantas tropicales como el kratom y la uña de gato. Este compuesto tiene una forma torcida única y podría combatir el cáncer, pero cómo las plantas lo producen era un misterio.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de UBC Okanagan descubrieron dos enzimas especiales que trabajan juntas para formar la estructura única de la mitrafílina. Dirigidos por el estudiante de doctorado Tuan-Anh Nguyen y la Dra. Thu-Thuy Dang, el equipo identificó cómo una enzima da forma a la molécula y la otra completa su formación. Este avance resolvió un enigma que había desafiado a los científicos durante años.

**Impacto** – La mitrafílina se encuentra en cantidades muy pequeñas en ciertas plantas, lo que hace que sea difícil y costoso obtenerla. Entender las enzimas involucradas significa que ahora se pueden explorar formas más sostenibles de producir la mitrafílina, sin depender de plantas raras. Esto podría ayudar a desarrollar nuevos medicamentos con este compuesto prometedor.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo de investigación planea usar estas enzimas para crear otros compuestos medicinales útiles. Trabajarán en adaptar el proceso natural para fabricar una gama más amplia de medicamentos, con el objetivo de mejorar la producción de estas moléculas valiosas.

**Frase clave** – Los científicos han descifrado cómo las plantas producen mitrafílina, abriendo la puerta a la producción sostenible de este raro compuesto contra el cáncer.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a twisting molecular structure glowing softly at the center, surrounded by delicate tropical leaves of Mitragyna and Uncaria plants rendered in natural greens and browns, with abstract representations of enzyme-like shapes gently guiding the molecule’s transformation, set against a soft, earthy background symbolizing scientific discovery and natural chemistry.

articles.title: Scientists reversed biological age in older adults with a 4-week diet change
articles.summary: A four-week diet change was enough to make some older adults appear biologically younger in a new University of Sydney study. Participants who reduced fat intake or shifted toward more plant-based protein showed improvements in key health biomarkers tied to aging. The strongest results came from a lower-fat, higher-carb diet, while people eating closer to their usual diets saw almost no change.
articles.content:
Older adults who cut back on dietary fat or reduced the amount of animal-based protein they consumed showed signs of becoming biologically younger, according to new research from the University of Sydney.

The study, published in Aging Cell, found that adults between the ages of 65 and 75 experienced reductions in their estimated 'biological age' after following specific diets for just four weeks. Researchers say the findings suggest dietary changes later in life may quickly improve markers linked to aging and overall health.

The research was led by Dr. Caitlin Andrews from the University of Sydney's School of Life and Environmental Sciences. While the results are promising, the scientists emphasized that the study provides only an early indication rather than definitive proof that diet can reverse aging. They say larger and longer studies are needed to determine whether these biological changes lower disease risk over time and whether the same effects occur in other age groups.

What Is Biological Age?

Chronological age measures how many years a person has lived, but biological age reflects how well the body is functioning. Biological aging can differ significantly from one person to another depending on factors such as health, lifestyle, and the body's ability to recover from stress and disease.

To estimate biological age, scientists analyze biomarkers, which are measurable indicators of physiological health over time. These biomarkers are often considered more useful than chronological age for understanding long-term health and potential lifespan.

For this study, researchers used information from 20 biomarkers to calculate participants' biological age scores. These included measurements such as cholesterol, insulin, and C-reactive protein levels. The data came from the Nutrition for Healthy Living study conducted at the University's Charles Perkins Centre.

Four Different Diet Plans Tested

The study included 104 participants who were randomly assigned to one of four diets. Each diet provided 14 percent of total energy from protein.

Two diets were omnivorous, with half of the protein coming from animal sources and the remainder from plants. The other two were semi-vegetarian diets, in which 70 percent of the protein came from plant sources.

Within those categories, participants were also assigned either a high-fat, low-carbohydrate diet or a low-fat, high-carbohydrate diet. This created four diet groups: omnivorous high-fat (OHF), omnivorous high-carbohydrate (OHC), semi-vegetarian high-fat (VHF), and semi-vegetarian high-carbohydrate (VHC).

Participants' BMI (body mass index) ranged from 20-35. All participants were non-smokers, non-vegetarians and had no serious complications (e.g., type-2 diabetes mellitus, cancers, renal or liver disease) or food allergies and/or intolerances.

Which Diet Produced the Biggest Change?

Researchers found that the OHF group, whose diets remained closest to what participants had already been eating before the study, showed no significant change in biological age markers.

However, the other three groups all showed reductions in biological age. The strongest statistical evidence came from the OHC group, which followed an omnivorous diet that was higher in carbohydrates and lower in fat. In that group, 14 percent of energy came from protein, 28-29 percent from fat, and 53 percent from carbohydrates.

Although the results suggest diet may influence biological aging surprisingly quickly, researchers caution that it is still unknown whether these improvements last long term or lead to sustained reductions in biological age.

Researchers Call for Longer Studies

"Longer term dietary changes are needed to assess whether dietary changes alter the risk of age-related diseases," said Associate Professor Alistair Senior, from the School of Life and Environmental Sciences and the Charles Perkins Centre, who supervised the research.

"It's too soon to say definitively that specific changes to diet will extend your life. But this research offers an early indication of the potential benefits of dietary changes later in life," said Dr. Andrews.

"Future research should explore whether these findings extend to other cohorts and whether the changes recorded are sustained or predictive of long-term outcomes."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific study showing that specific dietary changes can reverse biological age markers in older adults within just four weeks, indicating a promising, tangible health benefit with broad implications for aging populations. The study is detailed, focused on a single topic, and highlights potential for improving healthspan and reducing age-related disease risk, though it calls for further research.
articles.personality_title: Study shows diet change can reverse biological age in just four weeks
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists wanted to see if changing what older adults eat could affect their biological age. Biological age measures how well the body is working, which can be different from actual age in years. 

**What happened** – Researchers at the University of Sydney tested four different diets on 104 adults aged 65 to 75 for four weeks. The diets varied in fat, carbohydrate, and plant-based protein amounts. They found that those who ate less fat and more carbohydrates, especially with more plant proteins, showed signs of becoming biologically younger. The group that stayed closest to their usual diet did not see changes.

**Impact** – This study is important because it suggests that diet changes can quickly improve health markers linked to aging. For example, measures like cholesterol and insulin improved in just a month. This shows that even later in life, small changes in eating habits might make the body function better.

**What's next step** – Scientists say more research is needed to see if these benefits last a long time and if they reduce the risk of diseases related to aging. They also want to test if these effects happen in other age groups and with longer diet changes.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Changing diet to lower fat and increase carbohydrates and plant proteins helped older adults appear biologically younger after just four weeks.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une étude montre qu'un changement de régime peut inverser l'âge biologique en seulement quatre semaines
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Des scientifiques ont voulu savoir si changer l'alimentation des personnes âgées pouvait influencer leur âge biologique. L'âge biologique mesure le fonctionnement du corps, ce qui peut être différent de l'âge réel en années.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs de l'Université de Sydney ont testé quatre régimes différents sur 104 adultes de 65 à 75 ans pendant quatre semaines. Les régimes variaient en matières grasses, glucides et protéines végétales. Ils ont découvert que ceux qui mangeaient moins de graisses et plus de glucides, surtout avec plus de protéines végétales, semblaient devenir biologiquement plus jeunes. Le groupe qui a gardé un régime proche de leur habitude n'a pas vu de changement.

**Impact** – Cette étude est importante car elle montre que les changements alimentaires peuvent rapidement améliorer des indicateurs de santé liés au vieillissement. Par exemple, le cholestérol et l'insuline se sont améliorés en un mois. Cela montre que même tard dans la vie, de petits changements alimentaires peuvent aider le corps à mieux fonctionner.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques disent qu'il faut plus de recherches pour savoir si ces bienfaits durent longtemps et s'ils réduisent le risque de maladies liées au vieillissement. Ils veulent aussi tester ces effets chez d'autres groupes d'âge et avec des changements alimentaires plus longs.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Réduire les graisses et augmenter les glucides et les protéines végétales dans l'alimentation a aidé des personnes âgées à paraître biologiquement plus jeunes après seulement quatre semaines.
articles.personality_title_es: Un estudio muestra que un cambio de dieta puede revertir la edad biológica en solo cuatro semanas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos quisieron saber si cambiar la alimentación de los adultos mayores podría afectar su edad biológica. La edad biológica mide qué tan bien funciona el cuerpo, y puede ser diferente a la edad real en años.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Sídney probaron cuatro dietas diferentes en 104 adultos de 65 a 75 años durante cuatro semanas. Las dietas variaban en grasas, carbohidratos y proteínas de origen vegetal. Descubrieron que quienes comieron menos grasa y más carbohidratos, especialmente con más proteínas vegetales, mostraron señales de volverse biológicamente más jóvenes. El grupo que mantuvo una dieta similar a la habitual no mostró cambios.

**Impacto** – Este estudio es importante porque sugiere que los cambios en la dieta pueden mejorar rápidamente los indicadores de salud relacionados con el envejecimiento. Por ejemplo, mejoraron medidas como el colesterol y la insulina en solo un mes. Esto demuestra que incluso en edades avanzadas, pequeños cambios en la alimentación pueden hacer que el cuerpo funcione mejor.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos dicen que se necesitan más estudios para ver si estos beneficios duran mucho tiempo y si reducen el riesgo de enfermedades relacionadas con la edad. También quieren probar si estos efectos ocurren en otros grupos de edad y con cambios dietéticos más largos.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Cambiar la dieta para reducir grasas y aumentar carbohidratos y proteínas vegetales ayudó a adultos mayores a parecer biológicamente más jóvenes después de solo cuatro semanas.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of an abstract hourglass filled with vibrant, fresh fruits, vegetables, and grains flowing upward to replace dull, faded animal-based foods and fatty elements at the bottom, symbolizing the reversal of biological age through dietary change in older adults, set against a soft, natural background of gentle earth tones.

articles.title: Scientists discover hidden fat-burning switch that could strengthen bones
articles.summary: Scientists at McGill University have uncovered a hidden molecular “switch” that turns on a powerful calorie-burning system in brown fat — the body’s heat-generating fat linked to metabolism and weight control. The breakthrough centers on glycerol, a molecule released when fat is broken down in the cold, which activates an enzyme called TNAP and triggers an alternative heat-producing pathway that scientists had struggled to explain for years.
articles.content:
Scientists have uncovered a molecular "switch" in mice that turns on a hidden energy-burning system inside brown fat, a discovery that could eventually help researchers develop new treatments for bone disease.

The findings, published in Nature, provide new insight into how brown fat works. Unlike white fat, which stores energy, brown fat burns calories to generate heat. Scientists long believed this heat production depended on a single biological pathway. In recent years, however, researchers identified a second pathway operating alongside the original one, but they did not know what activated it.

A team led by Lawrence Kazak at McGill University's Rosalind and Morris Goodman Cancer Institute has now identified the molecular trigger for this alternative system, known as the futile creatine cycle.

Scientists Identify Brown Fat "On Switch"

When the body is exposed to cold temperatures, it breaks down stored fat to create heat. That process releases glycerol, a molecule produced during fat metabolism. Working with McGill structural biologist Alba Guarné, Canada Research Chair in Macromolecular Machines in DNA Damage and Repair, the researchers discovered that glycerol binds to an enzyme called TNAP in a region they describe as the glycerol pocket. This interaction activates the alternative heat-producing pathway.

"This is the first time we've identified how an alternative heat-producing pathway is activated, independent of the classic system," said Kazak, Associate Professor in the Department of Biochemistry and the Canada Research Chair in Adipocyte Biology. "That opens the door to understanding how multiple energy-burning systems work together to keep the body warm at the just-right temperature."

Discovery May Advance Bone Disease Research

Brown fat has attracted attention for its possible role in metabolism and obesity research. While the new findings could eventually contribute to those areas, the researchers say the most immediate significance may involve bone health because TNAP already has a well-established role in bone formation.

The TNAP enzyme is essential for calcification, the process that builds and maintains strong bones. Mutations that reduce TNAP activity can cause hypophosphatasia, a rare disorder sometimes called "soft bones." The condition can lead to fractures, chronic pain, and skeletal abnormalities. Certain inherited mutations have made the disorder more common in parts of Canada, including Quebec and Manitoba.

By studying TNAP mutations in laboratory experiments, the scientists discovered that the same molecular switch involved in energy-burning fat cells also directly affects cells responsible for bone mineralization and hardening.

The work builds on earlier research by McGill co-author Marc McKee and co-author José-Luis Millán of the Sanford Burnham Prebys Medical Discovery Institute. Their previous efforts helped produce a first-in-class enzyme replacement therapy designed specifically for hypophosphatasia patients with defective TNAP enzymes.

"This finding opens the door to a new kind of treatment, where increasing the activity of the TNAP enzyme through its glycerol pocket by natural or synthetic bioactive compounds could potentially boost the beneficial actions of the enzyme in patients, to help restore deficient bone mineralization to healthy levels," said McKee, Professor in the Faculty of Dental Medicine and Oral Health Sciences and the Faculty of Medicine and Health Sciences, and Canada Research Chair in Biomineralization.

Researchers have already identified dozens of possible drug candidates for future investigation.

About the Study

The study, "Glycerol-driven TNAP activation in thermogenesis and mineralization," by Mohammed Faiz Hussain, Lawrence Kazak et al., was published in Nature.

The project included collaborations with scientists from Queen Mary University of London, Northeastern University, the Sanford Burnham Prebys Medical Discovery Institute, and the Maine Health Institute for Research. Funding came from the Canadian Institutes of Health Research, the Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada, and the Fonds de recherche du Québec -- Santé.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough identifying a molecular switch that activates a calorie-burning system in brown fat, which has broad implications for metabolism, obesity, and especially bone disease treatment. The discovery of the glycerol-driven activation of the TNAP enzyme, essential for bone mineralization, opens new avenues for therapies for hypophosphatasia and potentially other bone disorders. The research is well-detailed, involves multiple institutions, and promises meaningful health benefits for a broad population.
articles.personality_title: Scientists find molecular switch in brown fat that could help treat bone disease
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Brown fat is a special type of body fat that burns calories to produce heat. Scientists have known about one main way this happens, but recently they found there is a second, hidden system that also helps create heat. Until now, they did not know what turns this second system on.

**What happened** – Researchers at McGill University discovered that when the body gets cold and breaks down fat, it releases a molecule called glycerol. Glycerol binds to an enzyme named TNAP inside brown fat, activating this hidden heat-producing system. This discovery explains how the second system works and is the first time scientists have found this molecular “switch.”

**Impact** – This finding is important because TNAP is also key to building and strengthening bones. Problems with TNAP can cause a rare bone disease called hypophosphatasia, which leads to weak bones and fractures. Understanding how glycerol activates TNAP could help develop new treatments to improve bone health by boosting the enzyme’s activity.

**What’s next step** – Scientists have already identified many possible drug candidates that might target this glycerol-TNAP switch. Future research will test these drugs to see if they can safely increase TNAP activity and help patients with bone diseases. This work could lead to new medicines that support both energy use in fat and stronger bones.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists discovered how a molecule called glycerol activates a hidden calorie-burning system in brown fat, opening new possibilities for bone disease treatments through the enzyme TNAP.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques découvrent un interrupteur moléculaire dans la graisse brune qui pourrait aider à traiter les maladies osseuses
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La graisse brune est un type spécial de graisse corporelle qui brûle des calories pour produire de la chaleur. Les scientifiques connaissaient un principal mécanisme pour cela, mais ils ont récemment découvert un second système caché qui aide aussi à créer de la chaleur. Jusqu’à présent, ils ne savaient pas ce qui activait ce second système.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université McGill ont découvert que lorsque le corps est exposé au froid et décompose la graisse, il libère une molécule appelée glycérol. Le glycérol se lie à une enzyme nommée TNAP dans la graisse brune, activant ce système caché de production de chaleur. Cette découverte explique comment fonctionne ce second système et c’est la première fois que les scientifiques identifient cet « interrupteur » moléculaire.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car la TNAP joue aussi un rôle clé dans la formation et le renforcement des os. Des problèmes avec la TNAP peuvent causer une maladie osseuse rare, l’hypophosphatasie, qui rend les os faibles et fragiles. Comprendre comment le glycérol active la TNAP pourrait aider à développer de nouveaux traitements pour améliorer la santé osseuse en augmentant l’activité de l’enzyme.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques ont déjà identifié plusieurs candidats médicaments qui pourraient cibler ce lien entre glycérol et TNAP. Les recherches futures testeront ces médicaments pour voir s’ils peuvent augmenter en toute sécurité l’activité de la TNAP et aider les patients souffrant de maladies osseuses. Ce travail pourrait mener à de nouveaux traitements qui soutiennent à la fois la dépense énergétique de la graisse et la solidité des os.

**Une phrase clé** – Les scientifiques ont découvert comment une molécule appelée glycérol active un système caché de combustion de calories dans la graisse brune, ouvrant de nouvelles possibilités pour traiter les maladies osseuses grâce à l’enzyme TNAP.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren interruptor molecular en la grasa marrón que podría ayudar a tratar enfermedades óseas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La grasa marrón es un tipo especial de grasa en el cuerpo que quema calorías para generar calor. Los científicos conocían una forma principal en que esto sucede, pero recientemente encontraron un segundo sistema oculto que también ayuda a producir calor. Hasta ahora, no sabían qué activaba este segundo sistema.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad McGill descubrieron que cuando el cuerpo se expone al frío y descompone la grasa, libera una molécula llamada glicerol. El glicerol se une a una enzima llamada TNAP dentro de la grasa marrón, activando este sistema oculto de producción de calor. Este descubrimiento explica cómo funciona el segundo sistema y es la primera vez que los científicos encuentran este “interruptor” molecular.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo es importante porque la enzima TNAP también es clave para construir y fortalecer los huesos. Problemas con la TNAP pueden causar una enfermedad ósea rara llamada hipofosfatasia, que provoca huesos débiles y fracturas. Entender cómo el glicerol activa la TNAP podría ayudar a desarrollar nuevos tratamientos para mejorar la salud ósea aumentando la actividad de la enzima.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos ya identificaron muchos posibles medicamentos que podrían dirigirse a este interruptor glicerol-TNAP. Las próximas investigaciones probarán estos medicamentos para ver si pueden aumentar de forma segura la actividad de TNAP y ayudar a pacientes con enfermedades óseas. Este trabajo podría llevar a nuevos tratamientos que apoyen tanto el uso de energía en la grasa como la fortaleza de los huesos.

**Una frase clave** – Los científicos descubrieron cómo una molécula llamada glicerol activa un sistema oculto de quema de calorías en la grasa marrón, abriendo nuevas posibilidades para tratamientos de enfermedades óseas a través de la enzima TNAP.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing brown bear-shaped figure made of soft, textured brown fat cells radiating gentle heat, with a delicate golden key shaped like a glycerol molecule unlocking a bright, crystalline bone structure nearby, symbolizing the activation of the fat-burning switch and its strengthening effect on bones, all rendered in natural earth tones and soft light.
